10	# Feature: DDx Agent Skills
11	
12	**ID:** FEAT-011
13	**Status:** Implemented (root `ddx` skill with nested workflow skills present in `skills/`, `cli/internal/skills/`, `.agents/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, and `.ddx/skills/`; site copy pending)
14	**Priority:** P1
15	**Owner:** DDx Team
16	
17	## Overview
18	
19	DDx ships a single root agent-facing skill (`ddx`) that makes any
20	skills-compatible coding agent "ddx-aware" after `ddx init`. Under that root,
21	DDx may ship nested workflow skills such as `bead-breakdown/`, `replay-bead/`,
22	`compare-prompts/`, and the bead-lifecycle quality skill governed by ADR-023.
23	The root skill and nested skills are written to the
24	[agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io) open standard so they work identically
25	in Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor, OpenCode, and any other
26	harness that implements the standard.
27	
28	When the user says "do work", "review this", "what's on the queue",
29	"create a bead", or any DDx concept, the harness discovers the `ddx`
30	skill via its description, loads `SKILL.md`, and routes via an
31	explicit intent table into `reference/*.md` files that contain the
32	domain guidance.
33	
34	## Problem Statement
35	
36	Prior iterations of FEAT-011 shipped ~7 sibling skills
37	(`ddx-bead`, `ddx-run`, `ddx-review`, `ddx-status`,
38	`ddx-install`, `ddx-doctor`). Real-world usage exposed problems:
39	
40	- **Intent ambiguity.** Users say "do work", not "/ddx-run". A flat
41	  list of named slash commands forces the harness to guess between
42	  `/ddx-bead` vs `/ddx-run` vs `/ddx-work` from natural-language
43	  phrases.
44	- **Vocabulary drift.** Each skill redefined terms (bead, queue,
45	  harness, review) inline; wording diverged across files and from
46	  FEAT-* specs.
47	- **Workflow-opinion leakage.** `ddx-bead` mandated `helix` labels
48	  and documented `phase:*` labels — HELIX methodology opinions
49	  inside a core DDx skill.
50	- **Skill tree drift.** Two copies of most skills
51	  (`cli/internal/skills/` embedded vs top-level `/skills/`) with
52	  real content divergence.
53	- **Init gap.** `ddx init` copied only 2 of 7 embedded skills; the
54	  rest never surfaced to Claude Code unless the user re-installed
55	  manually.
56	- **Portability.** Skills used Claude-Code-only frontmatter fields
57	  (`argument-hint`) that are silently ignored by Codex and other
58	  harnesses, and reached for Claude-Code-only patterns
59	  (`context: fork`) that don't exist elsewhere.
60	
61	The consolidated design fixes each of these.
62	
63	## Architecture
64	
65	### Root skill plus nested workflow skills
66	
67	```
68	skills/ddx/
69	├── SKILL.md                # overview, vocabulary, intent-router directive
70	├── reference/
71	│   ├── beads.md            # writing execution-ready beads (best practices)
72	│   ├── work.md             # draining the queue, execute-bead, verify + close
73	│   ├── review.md           # bead-review (AC grade) + quorum code review
74	│   ├── agents.md           # harness/profile dispatch, personas
75	│   ├── interactive.md      # default queue-steward conversation workflow
76	│   └── status.md           # queue state, doctor, health checks
77	├── evals/
78	│   └── routing.jsonl       # phrase→mode/reference/action fixtures
79	├── bead-breakdown/
80	│   └── SKILL.md            # workflow skill composition over ddx bead create
81	├── replay-bead/
82	│   └── SKILL.md            # workflow skill composition over ddx run / try
83	└── ...
84	```
85	
86	At harness startup, only the skill's `name` + `description` metadata
87	is pre-loaded. On activation, the harness reads `SKILL.md`. When the
88	intent router matches a phrase, the harness reads the matching
89	`reference/*.md`. Nothing else loads. This is the Anthropic
90	"Pattern 2: Domain-specific organization" pattern and matches how
91	Codex and other agentskills.io implementations progressively disclose
92	skill content.
93	
94	Nested workflow skills are independent skills stored below the root `ddx/`
95	directory so DDx can package them with the core domain vocabulary without
96	returning to the stale flat sibling model (`ddx-bead`, `ddx-run`,
97	`ddx-review`, etc.). They describe reusable workflows over DDx primitives:
98	break down an epic into beads, replay a bead under altered conditions, compare
99	prompt variants, estimate effort, run adversarial review, and perform
100	bead readiness assessment, lint/rubric scoring, and post-attempt triage. The
101	bead-lifecycle skill owns bead readiness assessment, the rubric pass inside
102	readiness, post-attempt triage, and refine guidance. They do not
103	introduce new DDx run kinds; FEAT-010's three-layer architecture remains the
104	execution substrate.
105	
106	### Portability contract
107	
108	Frontmatter is the portable-safe minimum — `name` + `description` only.
109	
110	```yaml
111	---
112	name: ddx
113	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "what's blocking the queue", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
114	---
115	```
116	
117	No `argument-hint`, `when_to_use`, `context: fork`, `allowed-tools`,
118	`disable-model-invocation`, `user-invocable`, `paths`, `model`,
119	`effort`, `agent`, or `hooks` fields — those are Claude-Code
120	extensions that Codex and others ignore or reject. The description
121	front-loads the DDx nouns (bead, queue, execution, harness, persona,
122	spec-id) **and** the exact verb phrases users say verbatim, because
123	implicit-invocation matchers prefer substring-ish keyword matching
124	over semantic understanding.
125	
126	### Intent-router directive
127	
128	Claude does not reliably auto-chase reference links; the router in
129	`SKILL.md` is stated as an **explicit directive**, not a hint:
130	
131	> "Before responding to any DDx-related request, read the matching
132	> reference file below. The router is not optional — your answer must
133	> be grounded in the reference file's guidance, not this overview
134	> alone."
135	
136	### Interactive-steward routing
137	
138	`reference/interactive.md` is the first stop for broad conversational DDx
139	prompts that combine queue orientation, planning, review, guidance folding,
140	spec alignment, and bead breakdown. It delegates to `status.md`, `review.md`,
141	`beads.md`, and `work.md` instead of duplicating their domain guidance.
142	
143	Router precedence is load-bearing:
144	
145	1. Explicit worker commands such as `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead
146	   `<id>`", or "start the worker" route to `bead_execution` and `work.md`.
147	2. Broad prompts such as "what's blocking the queue", "review with fresh
148	   eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "make this testable", and
149	   "break this down into specs and beads" route to `interactive-steward` and
150	   `interactive.md`.
151	3. Explicit code/doc edit requests such as "fix this bug" or "update this
152	   file" route to `direct_user_implementation`.
153	4. Explicit review-only requests route to `review` and remain read-only unless
154	   the user asks for fixes.
155	
156	`interactive.md` defines phase output contracts:
157	
158	- `orient` returns `QueueFacts`. It probes `ddx work focus --help`; if the
159	  command is unavailable, it falls back to `ddx bead status`, `ddx bead blocked`,
160	  `ddx bead ready`, and targeted `ddx bead show`. If `ddx work focus` exists
161	  but fails, the steward reports that failure.
162	- `plan` returns `SessionBrief`.
163	- `fresh-eyes review` returns `Findings` and `Verdict`. Multi-harness
164	  adversarial review is consent-gated unless explicitly requested.
165	- `fold guidance` returns `Accepted`, `Rejected`, `Unresolved`, and a revised
166	  plan.
167	- `align specs` returns `SpecDeltas`.
168	- `breakdown` returns filed bead IDs, parent/dependency edges, named tests, and
169	  verification commands.
170	
171	The route fixtures under `skills/ddx/evals/routing.jsonl` assert a stable row
172	schema: `phrase`, `mode`, `references`, `queue_commands`,
173	`tracker_mutation_allowed`, `code_edits_allowed`, and
174	`expected_next_action`. The evaluation driver consumes those fields directly;
175	it must not keep validating only the legacy `expected_reference` /
176	`expected_cli` pair after the richer schema lands. Negative fixtures are
177	required: "what should I work on next" routes to `interactive-steward` with no
178	code edits, whereas "implement the top ready bead" is direct implementation only
179	because the implementation verb is explicit.
180	
181	### Nested workflow skills
182	
183	Subagent orchestration remains harness-specific (Claude Code has
184	`.claude/agents/` + `context: fork`; Codex has its own subagent surface;
185	others differ). DDx does not ship harness-specific subagent definitions.
186	
187	DDx does ship nested workflow skills under the root skill tree when a
188	workflow has enough procedure to deserve its own reusable instructions. Current
189	examples include `bead-breakdown/`, `replay-bead/`, `compare-prompts/`,
190	`benchmark-suite/`, `effort-estimate/`, and `adversarial-review/`. ADR-023
191	(`../../02-design/adr/ADR-023-bead-lifecycle-quality-policy.md`) adds the same
192	model for bead-lifecycle quality: bead readiness assessment is the canonical
193	pre-claim decision, lint/rubric scoring is the rubric pass inside readiness,
194	and post-attempt triage is the after-evidence classification. Those steps
195	invoke a nested workflow skill while keeping routing through the root `ddx`
196	skill.
197	
198	Nested workflow skills are compositions over FEAT-010's three layers. They may
199	tell the harness to run `ddx run`, `ddx try`, or `ddx work`, but they do not
200	create a fourth run layer, bespoke storage shape, or harness-specific
201	frontmatter contract.
202	
203	The bead-lifecycle skill owns bead readiness assessment, lint/rubric scoring,
204	post-attempt triage, and refine guidance for this policy surface. Those
205	responsibilities are separate from the legacy `MODE: intake` compatibility
206	wording, which remains only as an implementation detail for older hooks.
207	
208	### Installation
209	
210	> See FEAT-015 (2026-04-29 amendment) for the authoritative install
211	> model: project-local copy semantics, zero symlinks created by
212	> `ddx install`, and the no-`~` manifest invariant.
213	
214	- `ddx init` copies `skills/ddx/` into `.claude/skills/ddx/`,
215	  `.agents/skills/ddx/`, and `.ddx/skills/ddx/` as real files
216	  (symlinks break after `git clone` on a fresh machine, and DDx no
217	  longer creates symlinks during install — see FEAT-015).
218	- DDx maintains five shipped copies of the root skill tree:
219	  `skills/ddx/` as source, `cli/internal/skills/ddx/` for `go:embed`,
220	  `.agents/skills/ddx/` for Codex and agentskills-compatible harnesses,
221	  `.claude/skills/ddx/` for Claude Code, and `.ddx/skills/ddx/` as the
222	  project-local DDx-managed copy.
223	- On init and on `ddx update`, stale ddx-prefixed skill directories
224	  from prior DDx versions are removed:
225	  `ddx-bead`, `ddx-run`, `ddx-review`, `ddx-status`,
226	  `ddx-doctor`, `ddx-install`, `ddx-release`. Third-party skills are
227	  untouched.
228	- Skills embed into the binary via `//go:embed all:ddx` against a
229	  copy under `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`. Because `go:embed` cannot
230	  traverse upward, a `make copy-skills` target rsyncs
231	  `skills/ddx/` → `cli/internal/skills/ddx/` before every build.
232	  That target covers only the embedded copy.
233	- Source skill edits that affect shipped interactive behavior must also refresh
234	  the project-local copies under `.agents/skills/ddx/`, `.claude/skills/ddx/`,
235	  and `.ddx/skills/ddx/` through `ddx init` / `ddx update` behavior or an
236	  explicit repo-local sync command. Tests must compare touched files across all
237	  five shipped paths so a green `make copy-skills` cannot mask stale project
238	  copies.
239	- The five shipped `ddx` skill paths are:
240	  `skills/ddx/` (source), `cli/internal/skills/ddx/` (embedded copy),
241	  `.agents/skills/ddx/` (Codex/agentskills project copy),
242	  `.claude/skills/ddx/` (Claude Code project copy), and
243	  `.ddx/skills/ddx/` (DDx-managed project copy). Nested workflow skills
244	  live under each copied tree so lifecycle hooks and human-invoked skills
245	  see the same instructions.
246	
247	### AGENTS.md: merge, not clobber
248	
249	Codex treats `AGENTS.md` as primary guidance before work, and users
250	may have added content. `ddx init` uses marker-delimited injection:
251	
252	```markdown
253	<!-- DDX-AGENTS:START -->
254	This project uses DDx. Use the `ddx` skill for beads, work, review,
255	agents, and status.
256	
257	(default interactive sessions use interactive-steward / `queue_steward`; explicit
258	`ddx work` and `ddx try` prompts use bead_execution only for executing bead AC;
259	tracker, merge, commit, safety, and verification policy still apply)
260	
261	(tracker/merge policy follows)
262	<!-- DDX-AGENTS:END -->
263	```
264	
265	Content outside the markers is preserved. The block says
266	"the `ddx` skill", not "`/ddx`" (which is Claude-specific slash-
267	command phrasing). Re-running `ddx init` updates the block in place.
268	
269	### Evaluation-driven validation
270	
271	Anthropic's skill-authoring guidance treats evaluations as
272	load-bearing, not optional. The repo ships:
273	
274	- `skills/ddx/evals/routing.jsonl` — at least 15 rows, each a user phrase plus
275	  mode, references, queue commands, mutation permissions, and expected next
276	  action, covering every intent-router entry and edge phrasing.
277	- `scripts/eval-skill.sh` — driver that validates the fixture schema, runs each
278	  row against `--harness claude` and `--harness codex`, and verifies the richer
279	  routing contract. `--validate` mode does agentskills.io spec conformance.
280	- `make eval-skill` in CI on PRs that touch `skills/ddx/`.
281	
282	## Requirements
283	
284	### Functional
285	
286	1. DDx ships exactly one root skill tree (`ddx`) in `skills/ddx/`; no sibling
287	   `ddx-*` skill directories. Workflow-specific DDx skills live as nested
288	   subdirectories under the root tree.
289	2. `SKILL.md` frontmatter contains only `name` and `description`.
290	3. `SKILL.md` body is under 500 lines and includes an explicit
291	   intent-router directive.
292	4. `reference/*.md` files are linked one level deep from the root `SKILL.md`;
293	   nested workflow skills carry their own `SKILL.md` and optional local
294	   `reference/`, `scripts/`, or `evals/` as needed.
295	5. `ddx init` copies the skill, removes stale `ddx-*` dirs, and
296	   merges the AGENTS.md block without clobbering user content.
297	6. `ddx update` refreshes `.claude/skills/ddx/` and removes stale
298	   dirs.
299	7. `skills/ddx/evals/routing.jsonl` contains at least 15 rows, each
300	   passing the richer route schema and then passing against `claude` and
301	   `codex` harnesses via `make eval-skill`.
302	8. `SKILL.md` passes `scripts/eval-skill.sh --validate` (agentskills.io
303	   spec conformance).
304	9. `reference/interactive.md` is present in every shipped skill copy and defines
305	   the interactive-steward loop, phase output contracts, mutation policy,
306	   consent-gated adversarial review, and `ddx work focus` fallback behavior.
307	10. Generated AGENTS guidance uses the same precedence as the skill: broad
308	    interactive prompts route to `interactive-steward`, explicit worker prompts
309	    route to `bead_execution`, and `DDX_MODE=bead_execution` never overrides
310	    tracker, merge, safety, commit, or verification policy.
311	11. `scripts/eval-skill.sh` consumes the interactive routing fields
312	    (`mode`, `references`, `queue_commands`, `tracker_mutation_allowed`,
313	    `code_edits_allowed`, `expected_next_action`) instead of relying only on
314	    the legacy `expected_reference` / `expected_cli` fields.
315	12. Tests or validation commands prove touched files match across
316	    `skills/ddx/`, `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`, `.agents/skills/ddx/`,
317	    `.claude/skills/ddx/`, and `.ddx/skills/ddx/`.
318	
319	### Non-Functional
320	
321	- Skills work with any agent supporting the agentskills.io standard;
322	  no Claude-Code-only frontmatter or directives.
323	- Skills are plain Markdown — no runtime dependencies.
324	- Skills degrade gracefully if DDx CLI is not installed (clear error).
325	- HELIX-specific rules (`helix` label requirement, `phase:*`
326	  enumeration) do not appear in the `ddx` skill; HELIX opinions ship
327	  in the HELIX plugin.
328	
329	## User Stories
330	
331	### US-110: Harness routes natural-language DDx intent
332	**As a** user in a DDx project using Claude Code, Codex, or any other
333	skills-compatible harness
334	**I want** phrases like "do work", "drain the queue", "review this",
335	"what's on the queue", "create a bead" to route to DDx guidance
336	**So that** I don't have to remember slash-command names
337	
338	**Acceptance Criteria:**
339	- Running `make eval-skill` passes all rows against both `--harness claude`
340	  and `--harness codex`.
341	- Each intent-router entry in `SKILL.md` has at least one matching
342	  row in `routing.jsonl`.
343	- Each row includes `mode`, `references`, `queue_commands`,
344	  `tracker_mutation_allowed`, `code_edits_allowed`, and
345	  `expected_next_action`, and the eval driver validates those fields.
346	- Route fixtures cover "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance",
347	  "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable",
348	  "what should I work on next", "implement the top ready bead", and
349	  `ddx work --once`.
350	
351	### US-110a: Interactive steward supports multi-turn planning
352	**As a** user steering a DDx project interactively
353	**I want** broad planning/review/breakdown phrases to follow a standard steward
354	loop
355	**So that** I do not have to retype "fresh-eyes review", "fold guidance",
356	"align specs", and "make testable beads" instructions every turn
357	
358	**Acceptance Criteria:**
359	- `reference/interactive.md` documents the session brief fields and phase output
360	  contracts for orient, plan, fresh-eyes review, fold guidance, align specs, and
361	  breakdown.
362	- "Fresh eyes" defaults to local structured review; adversarial multi-harness
363	  review is suggested and consent-gated unless explicitly requested.
364	- Durable bead/spec outputs inline the accepted session brief and never rely on
365	  chat history or `/tmp` files.
366	
367	### US-111: Skill stays under the token budget
368	**As a** harness loading the `ddx` skill
369	**I want** `SKILL.md` body under 500 lines
370	**So that** skill activation stays within the Anthropic-recommended
371	token budget and doesn't compete with conversation context
372	
373	**Acceptance Criteria:**
374	- `wc -l skills/ddx/SKILL.md` < 500.
375	- `scripts/eval-skill.sh --validate` passes.
376	
377	### US-112: `ddx init` handles existing projects cleanly
378	**As a** user upgrading from an older DDx version with
379	`.claude/skills/ddx-run/` and similar dirs already present
380	**I want** `ddx init` to remove the old dirs and install only the
381	  new root-skill layout
382	**So that** the harness doesn't see stale, conflicting skills
383	
384	**Acceptance Criteria:**
385	- In a dir pre-seeded with
386	  `.claude/skills/{ddx-run,ddx-doctor,ddx-bead}/`, running
387	  `ddx init` leaves only `.claude/skills/ddx/`.
388	- Third-party skills under `.claude/skills/` are untouched.
389	
390	### US-113: `AGENTS.md` merge preserves user content
391	**As a** user who has added content to `AGENTS.md`
392	**I want** `ddx init` to inject the DDx block without clobbering
393	what I wrote
394	**So that** my Codex / Claude / Gemini setup isn't broken
395	
396	**Acceptance Criteria:**
397	- Given an `AGENTS.md` with user content both before and after the
398	  `<!-- DDX-AGENTS:START -->` / `<!-- DDX-AGENTS:END -->` markers,
399	  running `ddx init` updates the block between markers and preserves
400	  everything outside.
401	- Running `ddx init` a second time does not duplicate the block.
402	
403	### US-114: `ddx update` refreshes skills
404	**As a** user who ran `ddx init` on an older DDx version
405	**I want** `ddx update` to refresh `.claude/skills/ddx/` to the
406	current shipped content
407	**So that** I don't have to re-run `ddx init` to pick up skill
408	improvements
409	
410	**Acceptance Criteria:**
411	- After `ddx update`, `.claude/skills/ddx/` bytes match the embedded
412	  skill content.
413	- Stale `ddx-*` dirs are removed as in US-112.
414	
415	## Dependencies
416	
417	- FEAT-001 (CLI commands the skill wraps)
418	- FEAT-006 (agent service — harnesses, profiles, personas)
419	- FEAT-009 (registry for package-install guidance inside `reference/agents.md`)
420	- ADR-023 (bead-lifecycle quality policy using nested workflow skills)
421	- agentskills.io open standard
422	
423	## Out of Scope
424	
425	- Workflow-specific skills (HELIX provides `helix-*` in its own plugin).
426	- Claude-Code-specific skill features (`context: fork`, `allowed-tools`,
427	  `paths`, subagents under `.claude/agents/`) — portability > optimization.
428	- A CI-enforced vocabulary drift guard. One skill + one glossary is
429	  self-policing. Revisit only if drift recurs.
430	
431	## Implementation Notes
432	
433	The migration from the 7-sibling-skills layout to the root `ddx`
434	skill tree is sequenced into phases; see the Phase 1 / Phase 2 / Phase 3
435	epic beads for the work breakdown. Phase 1 is the critical path
436	(ship the new surface + eval suite + init/update changes); Phase 2
437	is cleanup of old references; Phase 3 is the persona roster trim
438	(FEAT-006 scope, not blocking this feature).

Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.

5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
11	orchestrates work while Fizeau routes providers/models, catalog lookups,
12	and transcript rendering), a persona system (bindable AI
13	personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
14	personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
15	skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
16	CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.
17	
18	## How this skill works
19	
20	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
21	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
22	
23	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
24	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
25	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
26	guidance, not this overview alone.**
27	
28	## Vocabulary
29	
30	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
31	exact definitions.
32	
33	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
34	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
35	- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
36	  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
37	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
38	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
39	- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
40	  `ddx bead blocked`.
41	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
42	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
43	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
44	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
45	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
46	- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
47	  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
48	  passthrough constraints.
49	- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
50	  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
51	  finalization.
52	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
53	  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
54	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
55	  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
56	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
57	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
58	  below).
59	- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
60	  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
61	  branch on harness names.
62	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
63	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
64	  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
65	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
66	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
67	  personas.
68	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
69	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
70	- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
71	  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
72	  Fizeau owns the concrete harness/provider/model routing.
73	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
74	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
75	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
76	  `ddx install <name>`.
77	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
78	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
79	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
80	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
81	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
82	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
83	  orchestration; that remains harness business.
84	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
85	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
86	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
87	  `ddx upgrade`.
88	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
89	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
90	  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
91	  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
92	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
93	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
94	  via `spec-id`.
95	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
96	  at its governing artifact.
97	
98	## Intent router
99	
100	Before responding, read the matching file.
101	
102	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
103	|---|---|
104	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
105	| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
106	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
107	| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
108	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
109	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
110	
111	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
112	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
113	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
114	
115	## Top-level policy reminders
116	
117	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
118	reference file; do not violate them.
119	
120	- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
121	  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
122	  bead history and cannot be audited.
123	- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
124	  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
125	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
126	  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
127	- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
128	  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
129	  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
130	  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
131	  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
132	  branches.
133	- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
134	  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
135	  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
136	  manual parallel work should too.
137	- **Power-first execution dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
138	  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
139	  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.
140	
141	## Links out
142	
143	- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
144	- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
145	  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
146	  executions, and skills features.
147	- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
148	  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
149	- Open standard this skill conforms to:
150	  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).
151

1	# Status — Queue State and Health
2	
3	Three distinct intents live here. They answer different questions
4	and have different commands; call them by intent, not
5	interchangeably.
6	
7	## What's on the queue?
8	
9	"What's ready to work on?" "What's blocked?"
10	
11	```bash
12	ddx bead ready      # beads with all deps satisfied (actionable)
13	ddx bead blocked    # beads with at least one unclosed dep
14	ddx bead status     # aggregate counts: Open / Closed / Ready / Blocked
15	ddx bead list       # all beads (filter with --status, --label, --where)
16	```
17	
18	Typical answers:
19	
20	- "What should I work on next?" → `ddx bead ready` (top of list).
21	- "Why isn't bead X moving?" → `ddx bead show <id>` for full state;
22	  `ddx bead dep tree <id>` to see what it depends on.
23	- "How many beads are open right now?" → `ddx bead status`.
24	
25	## Am I healthy?
26	
27	"Is DDx installed and working?" "Are the harnesses available?"
28	
29	```bash
30	ddx doctor          # environment, config, install validation
31	# Fizeau diagnostics, if exposed by the project, remain separate from DDx.
32	ddx version         # CLI version
33	```
34	
35	`ddx doctor` checks:
36	
37	- DDx binary is reachable and recent.
38	- `.ddx/config.yaml` is valid (schema + required fields).
39	- `.ddx/beads.jsonl` is readable and parses.
40	- Git is available and the repo is in a usable state.
41	- Shell integration (where applicable) is set up.
42	- Plugins declared in config are actually installed.
43	
44	Fizeau diagnostics go further into the execution service: which
45	harnesses are discoverable, whether provider credentials are present,
46	and whether the model catalog loads.
47	
48	## Is the project drifted from upstream?
49	
50	"Has the DDx library changed since I last updated?" "Do I need to
51	pull new plugin content?"
52	
53	```bash
54	ddx status          # sync state: upstream revs, local drift, stale docs
55	ddx doc stale       # documents that reference outdated artifacts
56	```
57	
58	`ddx status` is the CLI-wide status: binary version, plugin
59	versions, document-graph freshness. `ddx doc stale` specifically
60	lists documents that depend on artifacts that have moved or
61	changed.
62	
63	## Don't use these interchangeably
64	
65	- `ddx doctor` — **environment health** (is DDx working on this
66	  machine?). Call after `ddx upgrade` or when things seem broken.
67	- Fizeau diagnostics — **harness health** (are the routes reachable?).
68	  Call when `ddx run` reports an availability failure.
69	- `ddx status` — **upstream drift** (is this project in sync with
70	  the library registry?). Call periodically to catch stale
71	  plugins.
72	- `ddx bead status` — **work queue state** (how many beads, in
73	  what states?). Call to understand project progress.
74	
75	A user asking "how's the project doing?" probably means `ddx
76	doctor` or the Fizeau diagnostics surface (am I set up correctly) —
77	follow up with `ddx bead status` if they want work-queue state too.
78	
79	A user asking "what's ready to work on?" is clearly asking for
80	`ddx bead ready`.
81	
82	## Anti-patterns
83	
84	- **Running `ddx status` to check if routes work.** Use
85	  the Fizeau diagnostics surface for that; `ddx status` is about upstream sync.
86	- **Running `ddx doctor` to see the queue.** Use `ddx bead status`.
87	- **Closing stale beads based on "status" alone.** `ddx bead
88	  status` shows counts, not quality. Use `ddx bead list
89	  --status open` + `ddx bead show <id>` to actually read each bead.
90	
91	## CLI reference
92	
93	```bash
94	# Queue state
95	ddx bead ready
96	ddx bead blocked
97	ddx bead status
98	ddx bead list [--status open|closed] [--label <l>]
99	ddx bead show <id>
100	
101	# Environment health
102	ddx doctor
103	ddx version
104	
105	# Agent/harness health
106	# Fizeau diagnostics, if exposed by the project, remain separate from DDx.
107	
108	# Upstream sync
109	ddx status
110	ddx doc stale
111	```
112	
113	Full flag list: `ddx doctor --help`,
114	`ddx bead --help`.
115

1	# Work — Drain the Queue, Execute, Verify, Close
2	
3	"Doing work" in DDx means draining the ready queue: pick the top ready bead,
4	run one or more `ddx try` attempts, verify the result, and close the bead on
5	success or leave it available for a future eligible retry.
6	
7	## Default surface: `ddx work`
8	
9	```bash
10	ddx work
11	```
12	
13	`ddx work` drains the queue by picking ready beads and invoking `ddx try`.
14	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`, which is the single agent invocation primitive. DDx
15	owns queue iteration, attempt evidence, and retry policy; the upstream agent
16	owns provider/model routing.
17	
18	Flags worth knowing:
19	
20	- `--once` — pick one bead and stop (don't loop).
21	- `--poll-interval <dur>` — continuous worker mode; wait between
22	  iterations.
23	- `--min-power <n>` / `--max-power <n>` — requested agent power bounds.
24	- `--top-power` — choose a `MinPower` threshold from the agent catalog.
25	- `--harness <name>` / `--provider <name>` / `--model <ref>` — passthrough
26	  constraints only. DDx sends them unchanged and does not route on them.
27	
28	## Primitive: `ddx try`
29	
30	For targeted re-runs, debugging, or running a specific bead:
31	
32	```bash
33	ddx try <bead-id>
34	ddx try <bead-id> --from <rev>      # base commit override
35	ddx try <bead-id> --no-merge        # preserve result, don't land
36	```
37	
38	`ddx try` runs one bead attempt in an isolated git worktree. It calls `ddx run`
39	for the actual agent invocation.
40	
41	## Pick by default, not by ID
42	
43	Under normal operation, **don't specify a bead ID**. `ddx work` picks
44	the top ready bead based on priority + dependency satisfaction. Only
45	pin a specific ID when debugging (`ddx try <id>`) or
46	when the queue ordering would pick the wrong bead and you need to
47	override.
48	
49	## Verify independently before closing
50	
51	**Agents hallucinate successful completions.** Do not trust the
52	agent's self-report. Before closing a bead:
53	
54	1. Run the acceptance-criteria command yourself (from the bead's
55	   `accept:` field). If it's `go test ./foo/...`, run that.
56	2. Check the resulting commit against the in-scope file list from
57	   the bead description. Out-of-scope files touched? Reject the
58	   attempt.
59	3. Read the commit message — does it reference the bead ID?
60	   (`[ddx-<id>]` or similar in the commit subject is the convention.)
61	
62	If all three pass: close the bead.
63	
64	## Close on success, unclaim on failure
65	
66	DDx try/work outcomes form a specific taxonomy. Each outcome
67	maps to a concrete follow-up action:
68	
69	| Outcome | Meaning | Action |
70	|---|---|---|
71	| `success` | Tests pass, AC met, commit landed | `ddx bead close <id>` |
72	| `already_satisfied` | No changes needed (AC was already green) | `ddx bead close <id>` |
73	| `no_changes` | Agent returned without producing commits and wrote `no_changes_rationale.txt` | Leave open, **unclaim** |
74	| `no_evidence_produced` | Agent exited without a commit or rationale | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate harness/commit failure |
75	| `land_conflict` | Merge conflict on landing the result | Leave open, **unclaim** |
76	| `post_run_check_failed` | Tests or gate failed after landing | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |
77	| `execution_failed` | Agent subprocess errored (timeout, crash, provider error) | Leave open, **unclaim** |
78	| `structural_validation_failed` | Result failed structural sanity check | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |
79	
80	`ddx work` applies these actions automatically. If you're running
81	`ddx try` directly, apply them manually: `ddx bead update <id>
82	--unclaim` to release a bead after a non-closing outcome, so another
83	worker can pick it up.
84	
85	**Never leave a bead half-owned** — every execution either closes or
86	unclaims.
87	
88	## Testing expectations for bead implementations
89	
90	When an agent implements a bead, its output should include tests
91	that exercise the new code:
92	
93	- **Unit tests** for logic with in-memory stubs for first-party
94	  collaborators. Favor stubs over mocks; mocks that assert on call
95	  sequences test implementation, not behavior.
96	- **Integration tests** against real collaborators where the cost
97	  is small (real git in a temp dir, real DB in a temp file, real
98	  HTTP via a local test server).
99	- **Real e2e tests** at the outermost boundary. E2e tests that mock
100	  the database or the network are unit tests lying about their
101	  scope.
102	- **Coverage measurement only where the project tracks it.** Don't
103	  introduce coverage tooling just for one bead; use what the
104	  project already has. Coverage is a signal, not a target.
105	- **Performance claims require baselines.** If the bead's AC
106	  mentions "faster" or "scales", the acceptance test must include:
107	  (a) a numeric baseline, (b) an explicit boundary (what's
108	  measured, what's excluded), (c) a reproducible harness.
109	
110	## Anti-patterns
111	
112	- **Trusting the agent's "done"**: always re-run the AC command
113	  yourself before closing.
114	- **Closing on no_changes/no_evidence**: only `success` and `already_satisfied`
115	  close a bead. `no_changes` requires an explicit rationale. `no_evidence_produced`
116	  means the agent returned without a commit or rationale — unclaim and investigate.
117	- **Squashing bead-attempt commits**: the per-attempt history is
118	  an audit trail (evidence commits, heartbeats). Use only
119	  `git merge --ff-only` or `--no-ff`; never squash/rebase/filter.
120	- **Running passthrough pins without a reason**: power-bound dispatch lets the
121	  agent choose an appropriate route. Use `--harness`, `--provider`, or
122	  `--model` only for explicit operator constraints, bug repros, or controlled
123	  tests.
124	- **Parallel workers on the same claimed bead**: the tracker
125	  guards against this via claim semantics, but don't try to defeat
126	  it — each claim represents an in-flight attempt.
127	
128	## CLI reference
129	
130	```bash
131	ddx work                                    # default queue drain
132	ddx work --once                             # one bead, then stop
133	ddx work --poll-interval 30s                # continuous worker
134	ddx work --min-power 10                     # request stronger attempts
135	ddx work --harness claude                   # passthrough constraint
136	
137	ddx try <id>                                # one bead attempt
138	ddx try <id> --from <rev>                   # override base commit
139	ddx try <id> --no-merge                     # preserve iteration
140	```
141	
142	Full flag list: `ddx work --help`, `ddx try --help`, `ddx run --help`.
143

1	# Review — Bead Review and Quorum Review
2	
3	DDx has two distinct review concepts. They are **not interchangeable**.
4	
5	| Concept | Command | What it does |
6	|---|---|---|
7	| **Bead review** | `ddx bead review <id>` | Grades a bead's implementation against its declared acceptance criteria. Per-AC verdict with evidence. |
8	| **Comparison/adversarial review** | workflow skill composition over `ddx run` | Dispatches review prompts across multiple roles or configured agent routes and aggregates the verdicts. Used for high-stakes or adversarial checks. |
9	
10	## Bead review
11	
12	```bash
13	ddx bead review <bead-id>
14	```
15	
16	Generates a review-ready prompt that includes:
17	
18	- Bead title, description, acceptance criteria (verbatim).
19	- Governing artifact content (follows `spec-id`).
20	- The diff since the bead's base commit (execution evidence commits
21	  excluded — see the related bug ddx-39e27896 for exclusion
22	  pathspec).
23	- Review instructions: output contract, verdict taxonomy.
24	
25	The reviewer (whoever receives the prompt — a human, `ddx run`, or a workflow
26	skill composition) produces a structured verdict:
27	
28	```
29	## Bead review: ddx-<id>
30	
31	### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
32	
33	### Per-criterion
34	
35	1. [AC text]
36	   Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
37	   Evidence: <file:line, test output, diff hunk, or explicit "not verifiable">
38	
39	2. [AC text]
40	   ...
41	
42	### Summary
43	<1-3 sentences of overall context>
44	
45	### Findings
46	<detailed issues, if any; omit when verdict is APPROVE with no findings>
47	```
48	
49	The review output can be stored as bead evidence:
50	
51	```bash
52	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body <path-to-review.md>
53	```
54	
55	## Comparison/adversarial review
56	
57	```bash
58	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review-prompt.md > review-code.md
59	ddx run --persona architect --prompt review-prompt.md > review-arch.md
60	```
61	
62	Aggregation policies used by workflow skills:
63	
64	- `majority` — at least ⌈N/2⌉ harnesses must agree on the verdict.
65	- `unanimous` — all harnesses must agree.
66	- `any` — pass if any harness APPROVES (rarely useful; adversarial
67	  checks should require stronger consensus).
68	
69	Use comparison/adversarial review when:
70	
71	- The change is high-stakes (migrations, security surface, API
72	  contracts, auth).
73	- The review surface is controversial and one harness's opinion
74	  isn't enough.
75	- You want adversarial pressure against a single-model blindspot
76	  (different model families have different failure modes).
77	
78	The aggregated output shows each harness's verdict side-by-side plus
79	the aggregate decision. Disagreements are surfaced, not hidden.
80	
81	## Required evidence format
82	
83	Both review kinds share an evidence contract: **no verdict without
84	evidence.**
85	
86	- "APPROVE" with no `Evidence:` lines → reject as malformed.
87	- "REQUEST_CHANGES" without pointing at the change needed → reject.
88	- "BLOCK" without a reason → reject (see the related bug
89	  ddx-39e27896 about `review-malfunction` retries).
90	
91	Evidence takes one of these forms:
92	
93	1. **File:line reference**: `cli/internal/bead/store.go:142`
94	2. **Test output**: `FAIL: TestBeadClose_UnclaimsOnNonSuccess (0.01s)`
95	3. **Diff hunk**: a short quote from the diff showing the offending
96	   or approved change.
97	4. **Explicit "not verifiable"**: the AC is written in a way that
98	   cannot be mechanically checked; flag this as a bead-authoring
99	   issue rather than silently approving.
100	
101	## Anti-patterns
102	
103	- **Hallucinated approval.** "Looks good to me!" with no per-AC
104	  grading. The review is worthless.
105	- **Reviewing the prompt instead of the diff.** The reviewer evaluated
106	  "what you intended" not "what you actually changed." Verdict must
107	  reference the diff or the resulting code.
108	- **Verdict without evidence.** Every verdict must have at least one
109	  evidence line; APPROVE is no exception (evidence can be "AC met:
110	  `go test ./foo/... passes` output attached").
111	- **Silencing disagreement in comparison review.** If two reviewers disagree,
112	  don't collapse to a single "decision." Surface the disagreement
113	  in the output and let the human decide.
114	- **Comparison review for routine work.** Single-reviewer bead review is fine
115	  for most beads. Reserve multi-reviewer comparison for the cases that justify the
116	  cost.
117	
118	## Subagents: not this skill's business
119	
120	Some harnesses can run a comparison in forked/isolated subagent
121	contexts; each harness implements this differently. This skill
122	doesn't specify how isolation is achieved —
123	`ddx run` is the portable invocation, and the harness decides whether to fork,
124	spawn, or run inline.
125	
126	## CLI reference
127	
128	```bash
129	# Bead review (single-reviewer AC grading)
130	ddx bead review <id>                           # generate review prompt
131	ddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude  # dispatch and grade
132	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body r.md  # store review
133	
134	# Comparison review (workflow composition)
135	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md > review-code.md
136	ddx run --persona architect --prompt review.md > review-arch.md
137	```
138	
139	Full flag list: `ddx bead review --help`, `ddx run --help`.
140

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# eval-skill.sh — validate the ddx skill and optionally drive routing evals
3	# against live harnesses.
4	#
5	# Usage:
6	#   scripts/eval-skill.sh --validate            # structural spec conformance only
7	#   scripts/eval-skill.sh                       # validate + routing eval on claude + codex
8	#   scripts/eval-skill.sh --harnesses=claude,codex
9	#   HARNESSES=claude scripts/eval-skill.sh      # env var also works
10	#
11	# Exit codes:
12	#   0 — all checks passed
13	#   1 — validation or routing check failed
14	#   2 — invocation error (missing files, no ddx binary, etc.)
15	#
16	# Validation (always runs):
17	#   - SKILL.md exists at skills/ddx/SKILL.md
18	#   - Frontmatter contains only `name` + `description` (portable agentskills.io minimum)
19	#   - Body is under 500 lines
20	#   - All reference/*.md files linked from SKILL.md exist
21	#
22	# Routing eval (skipped in --validate mode):
23	#   - For each row in skills/ddx/evals/routing.jsonl, invoke
24	#     `ddx agent run --harness <h> --text <phrase>` and check that the
25	#     response mentions the expected reference file OR the expected CLI command.
26	
27	set -euo pipefail
28	
29	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
30	SKILL_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/skills/ddx"
31	SKILL_MD="$SKILL_DIR/SKILL.md"
32	EVAL_FILE="$SKILL_DIR/evals/routing.jsonl"
33	
34	VALIDATE_ONLY=0
35	HARNESSES="${HARNESSES:-claude,codex}"
36	
37	for arg in "$@"; do
38	  case "$arg" in
39	    --validate) VALIDATE_ONLY=1 ;;
40	    --harnesses=*) HARNESSES="${arg#*=}" ;;
41	    -h|--help)
42	      sed -n '1,30p' "$0"
43	      exit 0
44	      ;;
45	  esac
46	done
47	
48	fail() {
49	  echo "FAIL: $*" >&2
50	  exit 1
51	}
52	
53	info() {
54	  echo "$*"
55	}
56	
57	# --- Validation (always) ---
58	
59	info "==> Validating $SKILL_MD against agentskills.io spec"
60	
61	[[ -f "$SKILL_MD" ]] || fail "SKILL.md not found at $SKILL_MD"
62	
63	# Extract frontmatter (between first two --- lines)
64	frontmatter="$(awk '/^---$/{c++; if (c==1) next; if (c==2) exit} c==1' "$SKILL_MD")"
65	[[ -n "$frontmatter" ]] || fail "SKILL.md has no frontmatter"
66	
67	# Check required fields
68	echo "$frontmatter" | grep -qE '^name:[[:space:]]*ddx[[:space:]]*$' \
69	  || fail "SKILL.md frontmatter missing or wrong 'name: ddx'"
70	echo "$frontmatter" | grep -qE '^description:[[:space:]]*' \
71	  || fail "SKILL.md frontmatter missing 'description:'"
72	
73	# Check for Claude-Code-only fields that would break portability
74	for forbidden in argument-hint when_to_use disable-model-invocation user-invocable allowed-tools context: paths: hooks: model: effort: agent:; do
75	  if echo "$frontmatter" | grep -qE "^${forbidden}[[:space:]]*"; then
76	    fail "SKILL.md frontmatter contains non-portable field '$forbidden' (agentskills.io minimum is name + description only)"
77	  fi
78	done
79	
80	# Body under 500 lines (per Anthropic progressive-disclosure guidance)
81	body_lines="$(awk '/^---$/{c++; if (c==2) { found=1; next }} found' "$SKILL_MD" | wc -l | tr -d '[:space:]')"
82	if [[ "$body_lines" -gt 500 ]]; then
83	  fail "SKILL.md body is $body_lines lines (>500). Move detail to reference/*.md files."
84	fi
85	
86	# All reference/*.md files linked from SKILL.md must exist
87	missing_refs=""
88	while IFS= read -r ref; do
89	  if [[ ! -f "$SKILL_DIR/$ref" ]]; then
90	    missing_refs+="$ref "
91	  fi
92	done < <(grep -oE 'reference/[a-z0-9_-]+\.md' "$SKILL_MD" | sort -u)
93	[[ -z "$missing_refs" ]] || fail "SKILL.md links to missing reference files: $missing_refs"
94	
95	info "✓ Validation passed ($body_lines body lines, portable frontmatter, refs exist)"
96	
97	if [[ "$VALIDATE_ONLY" == "1" ]]; then
98	  exit 0
99	fi
100	
101	# --- Routing eval ---
102	
103	[[ -f "$EVAL_FILE" ]] || fail "Eval fixtures missing at $EVAL_FILE"
104	command -v ddx >/dev/null 2>&1 || fail "ddx binary not on PATH; routing eval needs it"
105	command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1 || fail "jq required for reading routing.jsonl"
106	
107	info "==> Running routing evals against harnesses: $HARNESSES"
108	
109	fail_count=0
110	pass_count=0
111	IFS=',' read -ra harness_list <<< "$HARNESSES"
112	
113	while IFS= read -r line; do
114	  [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue
115	  phrase="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.phrase')"
116	  expected_ref="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.expected_reference')"
117	  expected_cli="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.expected_cli')"
118	
119	  for h in "${harness_list[@]}"; do
120	    # Skip harnesses that aren't available (avoid spurious failures in CI)
121	    if ! ddx agent list 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^$h"; then
122	      info "  (skip: harness $h not available)"
123	      continue
124	    fi
125	
126	    response="$(ddx agent run --harness "$h" --text "$phrase" 2>&1 || true)"
127	    if echo "$response" | grep -qF "$expected_ref" \
128	      || echo "$response" | grep -qF "$expected_cli"; then
129	      pass_count=$((pass_count+1))
130	    else
131	      fail_count=$((fail_count+1))
132	      echo "  ✗ [$h] '$phrase' — expected hint of '$expected_ref' or '$expected_cli'" >&2
133	    fi
134	  done
135	done < "$EVAL_FILE"
136	
137	info "==> Routing eval: $pass_count passed, $fail_count failed"
138	
139	if [[ "$fail_count" -gt 0 ]]; then
140	  exit 1
141	fi

input.content:
# Interactive Steward — Default Queue-Steward Conversation Workflow

This file governs broad conversational DDx sessions: queue orientation, planning,
review, guidance folding, spec alignment, and bead breakdown. It is the first
stop for any DDx request that is not an explicit worker command, direct code
edit, or explicit review-only request.

## Mode

**Mode: `interactive-steward`**

The interactive steward combines orientation, planning, review, alignment, and
breakdown into a coherent conversation loop. It does **not** implement ready
beads directly — that is `bead_execution` mode (`ddx work` / `ddx try`).

When a human says "what should I work on next", "what's blocking the queue",
"review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "break this down into specs
and beads", or "make this testable", they are talking to the steward, not the
worker.

## Router precedence

1. **Explicit worker commands** — `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead
   `<id>`", "start the worker" → `bead_execution` → `reference/work.md`. Stop;
   do not enter steward mode.
2. **Broad conversational DDx prompts** — queue orientation, planning, review,
   guidance folding, spec alignment, breakdown → **`interactive-steward`** → this
   file, then delegate to the matching domain reference.
3. **Explicit code/doc edits** — "fix this bug", "update this file" →
   `direct_user_implementation`.
4. **Explicit review-only requests** — "review this", "check against spec" →
   `review` → `reference/review.md`.

The steward delegates to domain references rather than duplicating their guidance:

- Queue/health queries → `reference/status.md`
- Review → `reference/review.md`
- Bead writing/breakdown → `reference/beads.md`
- Queue execution → `reference/work.md`

## Session brief

At the start of an interactive session, the steward collects a `SessionBrief`:

- **Focus**: the active bead ID or goal, if stated.
- **Horizon**: how much work the user wants to get through this session.
- **Constraints**: blockers, frozen areas, external dependencies.
- **Mode preference**: plan, review, align specs, or break down work.

The `SessionBrief` is kept in-conversation and referenced for all follow-on
actions. **Never write the brief to `/tmp` or a path outside the repository** —
if it needs persistence, embed it into a bead description or spec update.

## Phase outputs

Each steward phase produces a named output contract. Bead/spec outputs inline
the accepted session brief; they do not rely on chat history.

### orient → QueueFacts

Probes queue state. First try:

```bash
ddx work focus --help
```

If the command is available, run:

```bash
ddx work focus
```

If `ddx work focus` is unavailable (exits non-zero for `--help`), fall back to:

```bash
ddx bead status         # aggregate counts: Open / Closed / Ready / Blocked
ddx bead blocked        # beads with unmet deps
ddx bead ready          # actionable beads
ddx bead show <id>      # targeted inspection of the top ready bead
```

If `ddx work focus` exists but fails, report that failure — do not silently fall
back.

`QueueFacts` includes: total open, total ready, top ready bead IDs, current
blockers, and any error from the focus command.

### plan → SessionBrief

Given `QueueFacts`, produce a `SessionBrief` for this session:

- Which beads to attempt (ordered by priority + dependency satisfaction).
- Which actions are consent-gated (adversarial review, tracker mutations).
- What success looks like for the session.

### fresh-eyes review → Findings + Verdict

Structured local review of current branch or work-in-progress against bead ACs
or governing spec.

Returns:

- `Findings`: per-AC or per-concern items with file:line evidence.
- `Verdict`: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK.

**Multi-harness adversarial review is consent-gated.** Suggest it if the change
is high-stakes, but do not dispatch it unless the user explicitly agrees. Phrase
the suggestion as:

> "Would you like me to run an adversarial multi-harness review? This dispatches
> `ddx run` across multiple personas and costs more time."

### fold guidance → Accepted + Rejected + Unresolved + revised plan

The user provides guidance (feedback, spec change, constraint) to fold into the
current plan:

1. Parse each guidance item.
2. Accept items that align with the session brief and spec.
3. Reject items that conflict with governing artifacts (state the conflict).
4. Mark unresolved items that need a spec update or human decision.
5. Produce a revised `SessionBrief` incorporating the accepted guidance.

### align specs → SpecDeltas

Compare current implementation or bead AC against the governing spec (`FEAT-*`,
`ADR-*`, `SD-*`):

1. Read the governing artifact (follow `spec-id` if present on the bead).
2. Identify divergences between spec requirements and current AC or code.
3. Return `SpecDeltas`: which spec sections are satisfied, which are diverged,
   and what changes are needed to align.

If no `spec-id` is present, ask the user which governing artifact to align
against before proceeding.

### breakdown → filed bead IDs + dependency edges + named tests + verification commands

Break an epic or broad goal into execution-ready beads:

1. Produce a bead breakdown with title, description, and AC for each slice.
2. Each AC names at least one test function or `go test -run` filter.
3. File each bead via `ddx bead create`.
4. Wire dependency edges via `ddx bead dep add`.
5. Return: filed bead IDs, parent/dependency graph, named tests per bead, and
   verification commands.

Durable bead outputs inline the accepted `SessionBrief` and do not reference
`/tmp` files or chat history.

## Mutation policy

| Phase | Tracker mutations | Code edits |
|---|---|---|
| orient | no | no |
| plan | no | no |
| fresh-eyes review | no | no |
| fold guidance | no (unless breakdown follows) | no |
| align specs | no | no |
| breakdown | yes (`ddx bead create` / `ddx bead dep add`) | no |

The steward **never** edits code directly. It plans, reviews, aligns, and breaks
down. Actual code changes happen in `bead_execution` mode via `ddx work` /
`ddx try`.

## Queue orientation fallback

If `ddx work focus` is not yet available, use the three-command fallback:

```bash
ddx bead status
ddx bead blocked
ddx bead ready
```

Report the three outputs as `QueueFacts`. Note that `ddx work focus` is not yet
available so the user knows the fallback was used.

## Spec alignment

To align specs, read the governing artifact referenced by `spec-id` on the
current bead or task context. If no `spec-id` is present, ask the user which
governing artifact to align against before proceeding.

## Breakdown

A well-formed breakdown satisfies the 8-criterion bead-authoring rubric (see
`docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`). Each child bead must be
self-contained: a competent agent given only the bead body must be able to pick
a file to edit and run tests without asking.

Breakdown outputs include:

- Filed bead IDs (from `ddx bead create` output).
- Parent/dependency edges (from `ddx bead dep add` output).
- Named test functions or `go test -run` filters per bead.
- Verification commands per bead.

  row_count=$((row_count + 1))

      echo "  SCHEMA FAIL row $row_count: missing field '$field'" >&2
      schema_fail=1
    fi
  done
  # references and queue_commands must be arrays
  for arr_field in references queue_commands; do
    arr_type="$(echo "$line" | jq -r ".${arr_field} | type" 2>/dev/null)"
    if [[ "$arr_type" != "array" ]]; then
      echo "  SCHEMA FAIL row $row_count: '$arr_field' must be an array (got $arr_type)" >&2
      schema_fail=1
    fi
  done
  # tracker_mutation_allowed and code_edits_allowed must be booleans
  for bool_field in tracker_mutation_allowed code_edits_allowed; do
    bool_type="$(echo "$line" | jq -r ".${bool_field} | type" 2>/dev/null)"
    if [[ "$bool_type" != "boolean" ]]; then
      echo "  SCHEMA FAIL row $row_count: '$bool_field' must be a boolean (got $bool_type)" >&2
      schema_fail=1
    fi
  done
done < "$EVAL_FILE"

[[ "$schema_fail" -eq 0 ]] || fail "routing.jsonl schema validation failed"
[[ "$row_count" -ge 15 ]] || fail "routing.jsonl must have at least 15 rows (got $row_count)"
info "✓ Fixture schema valid ($row_count rows)"

# --- Harness routing eval ---

info "==> Running routing evals against harnesses: $HARNESSES"

fail_count=0
pass_count=0
IFS=',' read -ra harness_list <<< "$HARNESSES"

while IFS= read -r line; do
  [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue
  phrase="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.phrase')"
  mode="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.mode')"
  expected_next_action="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.expected_next_action')"

  for h in "${harness_list[@]}"; do
    # Skip harnesses that aren't available (avoid spurious failures in CI)
    if ! ddx agent list 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^$h"; then
      info "  (skip: harness $h not available)"
      continue
    fi

    response="$(ddx agent run --harness "$h" --text "$phrase" 2>&1 || true)"

    # Check if response mentions any reference file from the fixture
    matched=0
    while IFS= read -r ref; do
      [[ -z "$ref" ]] && continue
      if echo "$response" | grep -qF "$ref"; then
        matched=1
        break
      fi
    done < <(echo "$line" | jq -r '.references[]' 2>/dev/null)

    # If no ref matched, check queue_commands
    if [[ "$matched" -eq 0 ]]; then
      while IFS= read -r cmd; do
        [[ -z "$cmd" ]] && continue
        if echo "$response" | grep -qF "$cmd"; then
          matched=1
          break
        fi
      done < <(echo "$line" | jq -r '.queue_commands[]' 2>/dev/null)
    fi

    if [[ "$matched" -eq 1 ]]; then
      pass_count=$((pass_count+1))
    else
      fail_count=$((fail_count+1))
      refs_str="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '[.references[]] | join(", ")' 2>/dev/null)"
      cmds_str="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '[.queue_commands[]] | join(", ")' 2>/dev/null)"
      echo "  ✗ [$h] '$phrase' (mode=$mode, next=$expected_next_action)" >&2
      echo "      expected ref in: ${refs_str:-none}" >&2
      echo "      expected cmd in: ${cmds_str:-none}" >&2
    fi
  done
done < "$EVAL_FILE"

info "==> Routing eval: $pass_count passed, $fail_count failed"

if [[ "$fail_count" -gt 0 ]]; then
  exit 1

input.new_string:
# Copy the canonical top-level skills/ddx/ tree into cli/internal/skills/ddx/
# so //go:embed (which cannot traverse upward out of the package) picks it up.
# Runs as a prereq of build/test/dev so contributors never see stale embedded
# skills. rsync --delete keeps the destination in exact sync.
# Also refreshes project-local copies (.agents, .claude, .ddx) when they
# already exist, so interactive-behavior edits stay in sync across all five
# shipped paths without a separate ddx init/update invocation.
SKILLS_SRC := ../skills/ddx
SKILLS_DST := internal/skills/ddx
copy-skills:
	@if [ ! -d "$(SKILLS_SRC)" ]; then \
		echo "error: $(SKILLS_SRC) not found (run from cli/)"; exit 1; \
	fi
	@rsync -a --delete $(SKILLS_SRC)/ $(SKILLS_DST)/
	@if [ -d "../.agents/skills/ddx" ]; then rsync -a --delete $(SKILLS_SRC)/ ../.agents/skills/ddx/; fi
	@if [ -d "../.claude/skills/ddx" ]; then rsync -a --delete $(SKILLS_SRC)/ ../.claude/skills/ddx/; fi
	@if [ -d "../.ddx/skills/ddx" ]; then rsync -a --delete $(SKILLS_SRC)/ ../.ddx/skills/ddx/; fi
